title_en: Contracts and Financial Transactions in the Shafi'i School
title_ar: العقود والمعاملات المالية في المذهب الشافعي
content_en:
Mughni al-Muhtaj's chapters on financial transactions (mu'amalat) represent one of the most comprehensive treatments of Islamic commercial law in the Shafi'i tradition. Ash-Shirbini's commentary on an-Nawawi's Minhaj covers the law of sale, hire, partnerships, gifts, and a range of other contracts with a precision that reflects centuries of Shafi'i scholarly development.

The chapter on bay' (sale) begins with the three elements required for a valid sale contract: the contracting parties (the buyer and seller), the offer and acceptance (sighah), and the subject matter (mabi'). The Shafi'i school requires a formal verbal offer and acceptance for most transactions — unlike the Hanafi school's acceptance of bay' al-ta'ati (exchange without words) as valid by custom. Ash-Shirbini explains the Shafi'i position: the contract of sale is one of the most significant civil acts in Islamic law and should be made explicit through clear verbal expression.

The prohibition of riba (usury and interest) is foundational to Islamic commercial law, established by the Quran (al-Baqarah 2:275–279) with extreme emphasis: 'Allah has permitted trade and forbidden riba.' The Prophet's hadith in Sahih Muslim identifies six items — gold, silver, wheat, barley, dates, and salt — to which the two types of riba apply: riba al-fadl (an excess in exchange of identical goods) and riba an-nasi'ah (a delay in exchange that creates an obligation to pay more). Ash-Shirbini works through the applications of these rules to each of the six ribawi items and then extends the analysis by analogy to other goods that share the same 'illah (legal rationale).

The Shafi'i school's rationale for riba in the six items is: foodstuffs are ribawi because they are weighed or measured food items; gold and silver are ribawi because they serve as monetary media. This differs from the Hanafi rationale (which extends riba to all weighed or measured goods regardless of food status) and the Maliki rationale (which focuses on food staples). The differences in rationale lead to different scope of the prohibition: the Shafi'i school is generally more restrictive in its extension than the Hanafi school.

Gharar (uncertainty and deception) is the second major prohibition in financial transactions. The Prophet forbade 'the sale of gharar' (Muslim), and this hadith is the basis for the invalidity of contracts involving excessive uncertainty — selling a fish still in the water, selling a bird in the sky, selling fruit before it has shown signs of development, or contracting for the delivery of a specific item that may not exist. Ash-Shirbini defines the threshold of forbidden gharar with reference to how much the uncertainty affects the likelihood of contract fulfillment and how serious the consequences of non-fulfillment would be.

The bay' as-salam (forward purchase contract) is an important exception to the general prohibition on selling what one does not yet possess. It is permitted by a special text of the Quran (al-Baqarah 2:282) and the Prophet's approval: 'Whoever enters a salam contract should do so for a known weight, a known measure, and a known term.' Ash-Shirbini explains the conditions that validate a salam contract: the price must be paid in full at the time of contract; the goods must be described precisely enough to eliminate uncertainty about the type, quality, and quantity; and the delivery term must be specified.

The chapter on hire (ijarah) covers both the hire of persons (employment) and the hire of property (lease). The Shafi'i conditions for a valid ijarah include: identified parties, a specified benefit, a specified duration or task, and a specified wage. Ash-Shirbini examines the rights and obligations of employer and employee: the worker's right to wages upon completing the specified task, the employer's right to the specified benefit, and the rules for resolving disputes when delivery of the agreed service is disputed.
